coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of a peroxidase-like nanozyme and its application in visual assay for tyrosinase activity
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shuangqin
Liu, Di
Wu, Bingyan
Sun, Huipeng
Liu, Xiaoyan
Zhang, Haixia
Ding, Nana
Wu, Lan
coredata.dc:description: Both single-atom nanozymes (SAzymes) and protein-template metal nanoparticles have attracted comprehensive attention in several respects owing to their excellent catalytic performance, green facile synthesis process, and robustness. Herein, the peroxidase-like activity of single-atom copper anchored on bovine hemoglobin-template gadolinium nanoparticles (Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs) were successfully synthesized and two sensitive turn-on fluorescence strategies for tyrosinase (TYR) activity sensing were proposed for the first time. For strategy Ⅰ, TYR sensing was carried out from 1.00 to 7.80 U/mL with the detection limit (LOD) of 0.20 U/mL based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) and the in situ generated polydopamine dots (PDA-dots). For strategy Ⅱ, The LOD of TYR was 0.05 U/mL with the linear range of 0.40–19.70 U/mL based on the elimination of inner-filter effect (IEF) between FITC and the reaction product (RC) of phenol and 4-Aminoantipyrine (AAP). The smartphone-assisted sensing platform was applied to construct the on-site detection of TYR with both strategies. The developed probe possessed good selectivity and was successfully utilized to TYR detection in serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Single-atom nanozyme
Cu,Gd@BHbFITC
Tyrosinase
Fluorescence-on
Visual determination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of TYR in serum samples
Validation of the oxidase-like activity of Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs
The optimal components of peroxidase-like nanozyme
TYR sensing based on the Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs
Selectivity and reliability of the proposed method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs with the different scales: 10 nm (A); The full XPS spectrum (B); Cu 2p (C); Gd 3d (D); Gd 4d (E) of Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs.

The spectra under different conditions for the two strategies. (A) strategy Ⅰ, the emission of PDA-dots (a); the excitation (b) and the emission spectra (c) of FITC; the emission of TR + H2O2 + Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs before (d) and after (e) adding with TYR. (B) strategy Ⅱ, the absorption spectra of RC (a); the excitation (b) and the emission (c) spectra of FITC; the emission of phenol + H2O2 + AAP + Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs before (d) and after (e) adding with TYR.
Schematic illustration of the specific DA polymerization (strategy Ⅰ) and the generartion of RC (strategy Ⅱ) detection mechanism assisted by Gd@BHb, as compared to that of Cu,Gd@BHb NPs.

Fluorescence spectra of Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs towards various TYR concentrations based on the strategy Ⅰ (λex: 350 nm) (A) and the strategy Ⅰ (λex: 490 nm) (C); The corresponding relationship between the TYR concentrations and the ratio of F517/F439 (B) or the strength of F517 (D).

The captured fluorescent and colorimetric images by a smartphone for strategy Ⅰ (A, B, TYR: 1.00, 1.20, 1.53, 2.12, 3.15, 4.73, 7.80 U/mL) and strategy Ⅱ (D, E, TYR: 0.40, 0.79, 1.58, 1.97, 3.50, 7.00 U/mL), respectively. (C) and (F), The G/B and R/B ratios to TYR concentrations for two strategies by the scanning app (Color Grab). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The visual assay procedure based on paper microchip (A); and the images for TYR assay (B); R/B ratios to TYR concentrations for paper microchip by the scanning app (Color Grab) (C). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Selectivity of the proposed strategies for TYR detection based on the strategy Ⅰ (A) and strategy Ⅱ (B). (1) blank, (2) TYR, (3) Typ, (4) ChT, (5) HRP, (6) peroxidase, (7) PRP, (8) Lyz, (9) LPL, (10) BHb, (11) GOX, (12) HSA, (13) IgG, (14) NaF, (15) MgCl2, (16) ZnSO4, (17) NaCl, (18) CaCl2, (19) FeCl3.The concentration of TYR was 19.70 U/mL, and the others were 197.00 U/mL.
The assessment of the capacity of inhibitor screening based on the proposed probe to various concentrations of KA for the strategy Ⅰ (A) and strategy Ⅱ (B).
The sensing mechanism based on Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs for TYR determination.
TYR activity in human serums with the probe of Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: TYR assay based on strategy Ⅱ.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of a peroxidase-like nanozyme and its application in visual assay for tyrosinase activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Northwest Minzu University, Lanzhou, Gansu, 730030, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, Northwest Minzu University, Lanzhou, Gansu 730030, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Both single-atom nanozymes (SAzymes) and protein-template metal nanoparticles have attracted comprehensive attention in several respects owing to their excellent catalytic performance, green facile synthesis process, and robustness. Herein, the peroxidase-like activity of single-atom copper anchored on bovine hemoglobin-template gadolinium nanoparticles (Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs) were successfully synthesized and two sensitive turn-on fluorescence strategies for tyrosinase (TYR) activity sensing were proposed for the first time. For strategy Ⅰ, TYR sensing was carried out from 1.00 to 7.80 U/mL with the detection limit (LOD) of 0.20 U/mL based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) and the in situ generated polydopamine dots (PDA-dots). For strategy Ⅱ, The LOD of TYR was 0.05 U/mL with the linear range of 0.40–19.70 U/mL based on the elimination of inner-filter effect (IEF) between FITC and the reaction product (RC) of phenol and 4-Aminoantipyrine (AAP). The smartphone-assisted sensing platform was applied to construct the on-site detection of TYR with both strategies. The developed probe possessed good selectivity and was successfully utilized to TYR detection in serum samples.

A visual multimodal sensor for tyrosinase detection based on a novel peroxidase-like nanozyme.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Tyrosinase (TYR) is an important binuclear copper-containing oxidase, and can be mostly found in the cells and tissues of plants, animals and fungi [1,2]. It is available to participate the catalytic oxidation reaction of tyrosine or tyramine to phenol derivatives or the neurotransmitter dopamine, subsequently oxidize them to ο-quinones products and finally formation of melanin after oxidative polymerization with the assistance of oxygen molecule [3–6]. Meanwhile, our organism will be exposed to various diseases with the maladjusted expression of TYR. For example, albinism or vitiligo, is sensitive to UV exposure and risky to skin cancer when TYR is deficient [7]. Malignant melanoma occurs with the over-expressed of TYR [8]. Parkinson's disease (PD) and schizophrenia [9,10] are associated with the immune disorders, dopamine neurotoxicity and neurodegeneration. Thus, TYR is regarded as a vital biomarker, and it is valuable to develop a sensitive and feasible method to assess and understand TYR function in crucial biological and pathological processes.

Nowadays, a considerable number of reports have been emerged to provide suggestion for TYR activity detection. Such as electrochemical [11], electrophoresis [12], colorimetric [13], radiometric [14], and fluorescence methods [15–19]. Among them, fluorescent techniques have attracted more attention with their advantages of time-saving, easy-operating, sensitive, and non-invasive in TYR imaging and detection [20,21]. Review of the all reported studies, the sensing mechanisms can be classified into five categories. (1) With the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) phenomenon between the ο-quinones and fluorophore [15,22,23]. The substrate dopamine was first functionalized onto the surface of nanoparticles, with the addition of TYR, the dopamine was oxidized to quinone, and then the fluorescence of the corresponding nanoparticles was quenched. The TYR activities were related to the quenching fluorescent signals, but the turn-off response strategies were not ideal due to serious interferences. (2) With the 1, 6-rearrangment-elimination reaction [24–26]. The TYR recognition moiety, 3- or 4-hydroxy unit or boronic acid pinacol ester was linked to the fluorescent skeleton with no fluorescence signal, the TYR was added and facilitated the hydroxylation at the o-position vacancy, and thus led to the release of the 3-hydroxybenzyloxy group from the skeleton with the 1, 6-rearrangment-elimination reaction, and subsequently generated the new π-conjugated system with significant fluorescence signals. These strategies certainly achieved highly sensitive signal amplification detection of TYR, but the complex synthesis and purification processes were required. (3) With the unique covalent binding between the o-hydroxyl and boric acid groups [17,27,28]. The TYR activities were monitored with the ratio fluorescent or colorimetric UV–Vis signals, with limited selectivity and variation of pH. (4) With the remarkable chromo-fluorogenic reaction between resorcinol and catecholamines [18], or the reaction between catechols and naphthoresorcin (NR) [29]. They were available to achieve the dual-model detection of TYR, but were not applied to the in-vivo detection of TYR with a little tedious pH adjustment processes. (5) With in-situ formation of the fluorescent polydopamine nanoparticles, first proposed by Yang's group [19]. When methyldopa reacted with ethanolamine, an interesting green fluorescent polymethyldopa nanoparticles (PMNPs) were generated in-situ, and the emission intensity was proportional to TYR activity. Subsequently, Qiu's group developed a similar strategy of in-situ formation of PDA-dots catalyzed by Mn3O4 NPs [30]. They were indeed sensitive to TYR activity, but required longer polymerization time and the pH used was not sui for biological environment.

In recent studies, single-atom nanozymes (SANs), as a novel generation of nanozymes, have become star materials and attracted the keen interest due to their fantastic advantages in the field of catalysis and energy conversion [31–33]. They were widely applied in several fields such as biosensing, therapy, pollutant degradation [34–36]. Besides, protein-assisted metal nanoparticles with ultra-small size and decent biocompatibility, excellent stability, photoelectric performance, have been considerably applied in several fundamental fields [37,38]. Hemoglobin (Hb) is a typical hemeoprotein, with certain peroxidase activity, and easily undergo significant conformational changes after assembly with metals. The Hb-protected nanoparticles exhibit enzyme-like activity, optical and electrical properties with the mechanism of ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) or ligand-to-metal-metal charge transfer (LMMCT) [39–42].

Herein, Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs were prepared through the one-pot synthesis. The Cu2+ and BHb were applied to enhance the peroxidase catalytic activity, Gd3+ was utilized to provide abundant oxygen vacancy defects, and ensure more oxygen molecules loaded onto the surface of nanoparticles. FITC was selected as an internal fluorophore because its excitation wavelength was overlapped with the emission of PDA-dots and the absorbance of the products (named RC) of phenol and AAP [43]. The Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs significantly catalyze the oxidative polymerization of dopamine to PDA-dots and the generation of RC. As TYR converts tyramine to dopamine (DA), and phenol to quinone, the novel sensitive and high-efficiency TYR sensing platform was constructed, as shown in Scheme 1 .

All the chemicals and reagents were obtained from the following qualified commercial suppliers and were directly used without any further purification. Tyrosinase (TYR) and typsin (Typ) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Beijing, China). Bovine hemoglobin (BHb) and lysozyme (Lyz) were purchased from Beijing Biodee Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Chymotrypsin (ChT) and fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) were gotten from Mclean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Horseradish peroxidase (HRP), peroxidase, immunoglobulin G (IgG), human serum albumin (HSA), glucose peroxidase (GOX), r-prion protein (PRP), lipase (LPL), 4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tyramine (TR), ferric chloride (FeCl3), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), sodium fluoride (NaF), copper chloride (CuCl2), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), zinc sulfate (ZnSO4), imidazole-2-carboxaldehyde, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), 4-Aminoantipyrine (AAP), phenol, kojic acid (KA) were received from Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China). TYR activity detection kit was supplied by Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm−1) was purified by Milli-Q system of Millipore (Birrica, USA), and was applied to prepare all the solutions.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected with FEI Tecnai G2 TF20 Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) (Hillsboro, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was obtained with Axis Supra X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) (Kyoto, Japan). Infrared (IR) spectra were measured with Nicolet iS5 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR) (Waltham, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV–Vis (TU-1810) spectrophotometer (Beijing, China) with 1.0 cm quartz cell. Fluorescence spectra were gotten with RF-5301pc Fluorescence Spectrometer (Kyoto, Japan). The pH of all solutions were adjusted with digital pH-meter (PHSJ-3F, Leici) (Shanghai, China). The nanomaterials were purified and separated with refrigerated centrifuge (Xiangyi, H2050R) (Changsha, China).

The Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs were synthesized by a biomimetic mineralization strategy. Briefly, 100.0 mg of BHb powder was dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water, followed by mixing with 500.0 μL of GdCl3·6H2O (100.0 mM) under magnetic stirring at 37 °C. After 5 min, 1 mL of FITC (10.0 μM) was injected and stirred for another 5 min. Then, 500.0 μL of CuCl2 (100.0 mM) was added and mixed for 5 min, followed with several drops of NaOH (1.0 M) introduced to adjust pH to 9–10. With the precipitate gradually formed, the color of the mixture turned from deep red-brown to palm green. Subsequently, the reaction was allowed to age at 37 °C for 6 h. Finally, the product was collected by centrifugation, and washed with distilled water for several times. The solid powder was frozen in a freeze-drying oven for 24 h, and stored in dark for further use.

Typically, 10.0 μL of TR (10.0 mM) and 5.0 μL of TYR (788.20 U/mL) were added to 155.0 μL of HEPES buffer (10.0 mM, pH = 7.4). This solution was incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. Then, 10.0 μL of H2O2 (1.0 M) and 20.0 μL of Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs (10.00 mg/mL) were added to the above solution, and left at room temperature for 20 min. The mixture was transferred into a quartz cell to measure the fluorescence emission at 439 nm and 517 nm under the excitation wavelength at 350 nm, respectively.

The detection of TYR activity was conducted as follows. For strategy Ⅰ, 5.0 μL of TR (10.0 mM) was mixed with various concentrations of TYR in HEPES buffer (10.0 mM, pH = 7.4) at 37 °C for 1 h to form DA. Then, added with 10.0 μL of H2O2 (1.0 M) and 20.0 μL of Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs (10.00 mg/mL) and left at room temperature for 20 min after totally vortexed to form PDA-dots. Finally, the fluorescence emission data at 439 nm and 517 nm were collected under the excitation wavelength at 350 nm, respectively.

For strategy Ⅱ, 5.0 μL of phenol (10.0 mM) and TYR (various concentrations) were mixed in HEPES buffer (10.0 mM, pH = 7.4) at 37 °C for 1 h. Then, 10.0 μL of H2O2 (100.0 mM), 20.0 μL of AAP (10.0 mM) and 20.0 μL of Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs (10.00 mg/mL) were introduced and stand at room temperature for 20 min. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at 517 nm under an excitation wavelength of 490 nm.
Furthermore, the colorimetric method based on smartphone was also applied to detect TYR activity. The detailed procedure can be found from the reference [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TYR in serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The human serum samples of healthy volunteers were drawn at the infirmary of Lanzhou University. All samples were tested in accordance with the Laws and institutional guidelines for the use of human serum of Lanzhou University (Lanzhou, China), approved by the Ethics Committee of Lanzhou University, and with the prior informed consent of the human participants in the study. The obtained samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to remove the interfering macromolecules. The treated samples were employed to produce 10% diluted serum samples with HEPES (10.0 mM, pH = 7.4). The TYR activity were detected and then spiked the known quantity of TYR to one serum sample for subsequent recovery experiments as the aforementioned methods. In addition, the results were compared with those of TYR assay kit.

Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs were synthesized through the facile one-pot and enzyme-biomimetic approach (Scheme 1). In this process, BHb acted as a supported template for the formation of nanoparticles and load of FITC. The basic morphological characteristics of Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs were investigated, presenting 2–3 nm uniformly distributed spheres (Fig. 1 A).

The composition of Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs was confirmed with XPS, shown in Fig. 1 (B-E). The peaks of Cu, Gd were clearly observed in the full XPS spectrum diagram (Fig. 1B). Then, the detailed information of Cu and Gd were analyzed by XPS Peak 4.1 software, the two obviously peaks at 934.5 eV and 954.5 eV can be assigned to Cu (0) 2p3/2 and Cu (0) 2p1/2 [45], respectively, a weak peak at 942.7 eV was belong to Cu (Ⅱ) (Fig. 1C) [46]. Moreover, the peaks centered at 1191.6 eV and 1224.0 eV in Gd 3d spectrum were attributed to Gd 3d5/2 and Gd 3d3/2 (Fig. 1D) [47,48]. The two typical peaks of Gd 4d3/2 and Gd 4d5/2 (143.1 eV and 148.6 eV) were also appeared in the spectrum of Gd 4d (Fig. 1E) [49]. All the data were consistent with the previously reported literatures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the oxidase-like activity of Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the catalytic activity of Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs, we selected TR and phenol as the substrates for the strategy Ⅰ and Ⅱ, respectively. The normalized absorbance and fluorescence intensities were recorded (Fig. 2 A and B). The emission peak (439 nm) of PDA-dots (a) was overlapped with the excitation peak of FITC (b), which demonstrated that the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) was possible to occur between PDA-dots and FITC. A little peak (emission at 439 nm) was found in the mixture solution of TR, H2O2, Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs (d), which revealed that the Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs could catalyze DA to PDA-dots. After adding TYR into the system, the peak at 439 nm was decreased, and a new peak at 517 nm was appeared (e), which was consistent with the emission of FITC (c). It can be inferred in system (e) that a series of reaction happened: (1) TR was transformed to DA with the addition of TYR, (2) DA was polymerized to PDA-dots with the catalyst of Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs, (3) the FRET happened between PDA-dots and FITC. Finally, the fluorescence of PDA-dots was quenched and that of FITC was enhanced.

The absorbance peak of RC around 505 nm in Fig. 2B (a) was overlapped with the excitation (b) and emission (c) peak of FITC, which demonstrated the fluorescence internal filtration effect (IFE) can be occurred between RC and FITC. The peak at 517 nm was quenched in the mixture (d). After adding TYR, the fluorescence intensity of 517 nm was sharply enhanced in the system (e). It revealed that (1) phenol was oxidized to benzoquinone by TYR, (2) the reaction of AAP and phenol was refrained to form RC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimal components of peroxidase-like nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: The following possible hypotheses and the similar materials were considered to verify the probe for TYR activity detection. We chose TR as the substrate for strategy Ⅰ to examine fluorescence intensity with/without TYR.
The function of following components including Cu2+, BHb, Cu2+ + BHb, Cu@BHb, Gd@BHb, Cu,Gd@BHb to the formation of PDA-dots was investigated.

First, it did not produce a peak at 439 nm when Cu2+ was added to the mixture of TR and H2O2 regardless of TYR presence or not (Fig. S1A†), meaning that Cu2+ alone couldn't catalyze TR to PDA-dots. Second, replaced Cu2+ with BHb and added into the mixture, a peak at 439 nm was appeared but it was weakened after the addition of TYR (Fig. S1B†), which revealed that BHb was not conductive to the TYR detection. Third, when Cu2+ and BHb were added simultaneously (Fig. S1C†) to the mixture, it is found the signals were very weak and no change after TYR added, which demonstrated that free Cu2+ and BHb have no catalytic performance.

The material of Cu@BHb or Gd@BHb was added into the mixture, we can find that the peak at 439 nm showed a slightly red-shift after incubated with TYR but the fluorescence strength had no change (Fig. S1D†, Fig. S1E†). Encouragingly, when the Cu,Gd@BHb NPs was used to catalyze the system, the peak at 439 nm was apparently increased with the addition of TYR (Fig. S1F†). It manifested that the Cu,Gd@BHb NPs were able to effectively enhance the DA polymerization to PDA-dots.

Gd dopping can produce abundant oxygen vacancy defects, and ensure more oxygen molecules loaded onto the Cu,Gd@BHb NPs to significantly enhance its catalytic activity [50]. In addition, Cu dopping can be capable of catalyzing the generation of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) in the presence of H2O2 and further intensifying the catalytic activity [51,52]. Moreover, when BHb was encapsulated into or immobilized onto the corresponding materials, it also exhibited peroxidase properties. Hence, the three substances were assembled to generate the novel nanoparticle, with magically enhanced catalytic properties.

Then, we further carried out the corresponding calculation to speculate that Cu,Gd@BHb nanoparticles acted as nanoenzyme, providing more opportunity for DA polymerization (Fig. 3 ). The valence bands of Gd@BHb and Cu,Gd@BHb were firstly fitted according to their XPS spectra [53], which were 2.22 eV and 1.44 eV (Fig. S2A and S2B†), respectively. Next, we fitted the bandgaps of Gd@BHb and Cu,Gd@BHb through the UV–vis spectra data (Fig. S2C and S2D†), which were 7.49 and 6.20 eV, respectively. The conduction bands of Gd@BHb and Cu,Gd@BHb were finally calculated at −9.71 eV and −7.64 eV, respectively. Therefore, the conduction band of Cu,Gd@BHb was lower, which was caused by the coordination of BHb with Cu2+. In the Cu,Gd@BHb NPs, the bandgap was narrower than that of Gd@BHb and more light can be absorbed to promote electrons from the valence band to conduction band, and finally generated more electron hole and photoelectron to produce 1O2. The more generated 1O2 accelerated the polymerization DA to PDA, which was consistent with the experiment results (Fig. S1F†).

In order to explore the effect of the ratio of Gd3+and Cu2+ on the catalytic capacity of Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs, different Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs with the molar ratio (Gd3+/Cu2+: 2/1, 1/1, 1/2, 1/5) was investigated respectively. As described in Fig. S3†, with increasing the quantity of Cu2+, the catalytic activity became weaker due to more copper precipitation was produced and inhibited the formation of Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs. Therefore, the molar ratio of Gd3+/Cu2+ was selected as 1/1 in the following experiments.

The reaction time of the polymerization of dopamine to PDA-dots, and the conjugation time of benzoquinone with AAP had decisive effects on the fluorescence of PDA-dots and FITC. With the time prolonged, the fluorescence intensity ratio of F517/F439 was firstly increased and then decreased, and reached maximum at 20 min (Fig. S4A†). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity at 517 nm exhibited similar trend and the optimal time was 20 min too (Fig. S4B†).

In view of ZIF nanoparticles were commonly used as a template or carrier because of its large specific surface area, excellent dispersion and stability, and low toxicity, we synthesized the ZIF-90@BHbFITC and Cu,ZIF-90@BHbFITC respectively [44,54], and tested their catalytic activities. The emission intensity at 439 nm just had slight enhancement and the peak at 517 nm wasn't appeared in the spectrum of the mixture of TYR, TR, H2O2 and ZIF-90@BHbFITC or Cu,ZIF-90@BHbFITC (Fig. S5A†, S5B†). Thus the proposed assembly strategies here were superior to the immobilization of BHb into ZIF-90, because the chemical adsorption and coordination happened on Cu,Gd@BHbFITC, which was stronger than the physical absorption in the latter strategy [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: TYR sensing based on the Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs
ce:section.ce:para: TR/phenol could be oxidized to dopamine (DA)/quinone by TYR, and the processes were dose-dependent to the concentrations of TYR [29]. For the strategy I, as displayed in Fig. 4 A, with the increase of TYR (0–47.00 U/mL), the emission peak at 517 nm was gradually enhanced, while the peak at 439 nm reduced. The relationship of intensity ratio (F517/F439) to TYR activity was evaluated (Fig. 4B) with the linear range of 1.00–7.80 U/mL and the limit detection (LOD) of 0.20 ± 0.03 U/mL. For the strategy Ⅱ, with the amount of TYR enlarged from 0 to 78.00 U/mL, the fluorescence intensity at 517 nm was consequently increased (Fig. 4C) with the linear range at 0.40–19.70 U/mL (Fig. 4D) and the LOD as 0.05 ± 0.02 U/mL, which was lower than the former strategy. Compared with the methods reported (Table S1), the LODs are competitive to the other strategies [24,55–57]. The fluorescence probe based on resorufin has relatively high sensitivity, but with tedious synthesis and purification processes and long response time to TYR (3 h) [24] (same as the reference [5]†). The LOD was higher than Qiu's group reported [30] (same as the reference [8]†), but it needed 2.5 h to obtain the fluorescence PDA-dots and longer than our approach (80 min). Hence, it suggested that the developed approach was more likely to be applied to the TYR detection in the complex biological samples.

Moreover, TYR was detected on-site with the help of a color scanning App (Color Grab) to scan R (red), G (green), and B (blue) color intensities of the reaction systems. As shown in Fig. 5 , the color change was more obvious under UV 365 nm (Fig. 5A and B) for strategy Ⅰ but under natural light (Fig. 5D and E) for strategy Ⅱ, thus we chose different conditions to carry out the imaging analysis.

The relationships of the concentrations of TYR to the RGB signals were simulated, respectively. For strategy Ⅰ, the linear range was 1.00–7.80 U/mL with the G/B ratios to TYR concentrations and the LOD was 0.35 ± 0.06 U/mL (Fig. 5C). For strategy Ⅱ, the linear relationship was 0.40–7.00 U/mL with the R/B ratios to TYR concentrations and the LOD was 0.08 ± 0.10 U/mL (Fig. 5F).

The paper microchip-based visual method based on strategy Ⅱ was further applied to realize the point-of-care assay for TYR, and the detailed operation steps and corresponding images for TYR assay were described in Fig. 6 A and B. The colors of the mixture (phenol + H2O2 + AAP + Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs) were gradually turned to colorless after adding TYR, and the calibration curve for the method was summarized in Fig. 6C. The R/B ratio versus TYR concentration has a good linear range of 0.40–1.97 U/mL with the LOD of 0.12 ± 0.11 U/mL. These results certified that the proposed method can be feasibly applied to TYR determination based on RGB strategy. At last we selected strategy Ⅱ to detect TYR in serum samples with mobile phone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and reliability of the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of the proposed method was tested with various interfering substances containing enzymes, proteins and ions, as shown in Fig. 7 . An obvious ratio signal enhancement was observed for TYR, whereas other enzymes and proteins exhibited negligible effects, although their concentrations were 10-fold higher than that of TYR. Other ions also did not cause significant signal changes based on both strategies (Fig. 7A and B). These results demonstrated that this novel constructed sensing probe has great selectivity.

We selected kojic acid (KA) [18] as a model to evaluate the capacity of this proposed probe for screening TYR inhibitors and the inhibitor efficiency was recorded in Fig. 8 . It was found that the inhibition efficiencies were gradually enhanced with the KA concentrations increased from 0 to 1.00 mM for both strategies (Fig. 8A and B), and the IC50 were calculated to be 0.16 and 0.05 mM, respectively. The results were consistent with the previous literature report [18], and thus the novel approach has certain potential in screening TYR inhibitors.

To further demonstrate the reliability of the proposed approach for complex biological samples, the serum samples from healthy human were collected and used to monitor the amount of TYR. The mean amount of TYR of the blank samples were 1.10, 1.01 and 1.15 U/mL for TYR kit, fluorescence and RGB approach, respectively (strategy Ⅱ), which were consistent with each other (Table 1 , Fig. S6†). Sample 1 was furthermore selected as a model to conduct the recovery experiments, and the reliable recoveries were calculated varying from 92.0% to 101.0%, which suggested that the constructed probe could be utilized in biological matrices for monitoring TYR activity. The uncertainty limits of each group were severally calculated according to “Evaluation and Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement”---“JJF 1059–1999” drafted by National Technical Committee of Legal Metrology (China).

In summary, a novel composite Cu,Gd@BHbFITC NPs was synthesized with a facile one-pot approach. Such nanoparticle combined the advantages of Cu2+, Gd3+ and BHb, and exhibited excellent catalytic oxidation performance. Therefore, it was promising to serve as an effective nano-catalyst for sensitive and selective detection of TYR activity, with a low LOD of 0.05 U/mL. Meanwhile, it significantly shortened the reaction time, and can be used to quantitative analysis of TYR with RGB strategy based on smartphone. It was successfully applied to detect the amount of TYR in human serum samples, and the results were consistent with the TYR assay kit. Hence, the proposed approach can be considered as a potential candidate to create the convenient and fast TYR detection sensor for the early discovery and prevention of TYR-associated diseases.

Shuangqin Li: Scheme Design, Probe Synthesis, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Di Liu, Bingyan Wu and Huipeng Sun: experimental assistance. Nana Ding and Lan Wu: The article checks. Xiaoyan Liu: Grammar revision. Haixia Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22174058) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 31920200002) and the Key Talent Projects in Gansu Province.

Chem. 92 (2020) 1236−1244, https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.analchem.9b04384.
